params.text:
Your legal and professional framework

Treating people with privacy and dignity is not optional or a matter of personal style. Several frameworks make it a clear expectation of your role.

  • Human Rights Act 1998, Article 8 — the right to respect for a private and family life, home and correspondence. This protects a person's autonomy, their relationships and their control over personal information.
  • CQC Regulation 10 — Dignity and respect — services must ensure people are treated with dignity and respect at all times, supported to maintain independence, and never left in undignified situations.
  • The Dignity in Care campaign and its 10 Dignity Do's, hosted by SCIE, which set out practical commitments for everyone working in care.

Article 8 is a qualified right: it can occasionally be limited to keep someone or others safe, but any interference must be proportionate and justified — never a matter of convenience.

params.alt: Illustration for The law behind it

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Match each framework to what it means for your everyday practice.

dialogs.answer: The right to respect for private and family life. In care, it underpins privacy during personal care, control over personal information, and keeping people connected to family and community.

Dignity and respect. Providers must treat people with dignity at all times, honour their preferences (including who delivers intimate care), and never neglect or leave anyone in an undignified situation.
A set of practical commitments from the Dignity in Care campaign, including respecting privacy, offering personalised care, and helping people keep confidence and self-esteem.
Requires you to maintain privacy and dignity, support people's right to make choices, and enable their active participation in their own care.
